children.text: Purpose.
The 3-panel editor models a browser-native IDE designed specifically for structured lessons and classroom environments.
Authoring layer.
Markdown-based instructions render alongside an Ace-powered code editor, keeping content and execution tightly coupled while preserving clarity for students.
Execution layer.
A centralized execution router determines how user code runs based on context.
Web projects execute inside a sandboxed
iframe
, preventing cross-context leakage.
Python exercises run in-browser using
Skulpt
, eliminating server dependency while preserving safety.
Terminal output is surfaced directly in the interface, ensuring immediate feedback without breaking user flow.
Persistence layer.
Projects are explicitly saved using a keyboard shortcut and written to AWS S3, enabling consistent cross-device access for both teachers and students.
The architecture balances IDE-level capability with classroom constraints by isolating execution while remaining entirely browser-native.
Purpose.
The organization and license model separates user identity from institutional access control, enabling clean multi-tenant scalability.
Identity and ownership.
Users exist independently of organizations and can maintain personal projects regardless of institutional affiliation.
License enforcement.
Each organization is governed by a centralized license that determines classroom availability through an access gate.
A
valid license
enables full classroom functionality.
An
expired license
transitions projects into read-only mode without deleting or restricting visibility.
This model preserves student work while maintaining strict access governance across organizations.
Separating identity, ownership, and licensing created a scalable foundation for supporting multiple schools without entangling user data with subscription state.

children.text: Purpose.
This flow describes how users move from classroom context into active project work within the platform.
Role-aware entry.
Both teachers and students begin on a unified Classrooms page, but are routed to role-specific class lists before converging at a shared Classroom Dashboard.
Project resolution.
When a lesson is selected, a deterministic project resolver decides how work should be handled.
If prior work exists, the system opens the existing project state.
If no work exists, a new project is cloned from the lesson template and initialized with grading metadata.
This resolver ensured a predictable and low-friction transition from instruction to execution, regardless of user role.

children.text: Composable curriculum architecture.
The system models curriculum as a hierarchical composition chain from organization to lesson, enforcing structural boundaries between authoring and execution.
Structure is centralized.
Organizations define dashboards, courses, units, and lessons without duplicating executable content.
Execution is isolated.
Classrooms consume curriculum artifacts without owning them, protecting live environments from structural mutations.
Templates provide indirection.
Lessons reference stable templates, enabling safe updates across multi-organization deployments.

A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> A

children.text: Architectural intent.
This hackathon prototype demonstrates a state-aware, voice-driven repair assistant built using event-driven cloud architecture principles.
Input abstraction layer.
Technician speech is captured as a natural input modality and converted into structured text through a speech-to-text pipeline, abstracting physical interaction away from the workflow.
Intent resolution pipeline.
Transcribed commands are routed through an NLP processor and dispatched to AWS Lambda functions, where intent classification and contextual state evaluation occur.
State-aware orchestration.
The system maintains repair-step progression within cloud logic, enabling dynamic instruction resolution based on prior actions rather than static command-response mapping.
Instruction synthesis.
Parsed workflow decisions are transformed into structured procedural guidance and returned as audio output, completing the closed feedback loop without requiring manual device interaction.
Although developed within hackathon constraints, the architecture models a scalable, event-driven service layer capable of supporting real-world, hands-free industrial workflows.

NLP --> Lambda
Lambda --> Engine
Engine --> Audio
Audio --> Tech

children.text: A technician issues a voice command which flows through
capture, transcription, intent extraction, and execution
before returning spoken guidance.
Audio → Text
Text → Intent
Intent → Action
Action → Spoken Response

sequenceDiagram

participant Tech as Technician
participant Mic as Voice Capture
participant STT as Speech-to-Text
participant NLP as NLP Engine
participant Lambda as AWS Lambda
participant Engine as Instruction Engine
participant Audio as Audio Output

Tech ->> Mic: Issue voice command
Mic ->> STT: Stream audio
STT ->> NLP: Transcribed command
NLP ->> Lambda: Intent + extracted entities
Lambda ->> Engine: Structured action request
Engine ->> Audio: Instruction payload
Audio -->> Tech: Spoken guidance

children.text: This diagram models the
end-to-end lifecycle
of a technician-issued voice command within the system.
Voice Capture
streams raw audio from the technician.
Speech-to-Text
converts audio into structured text.
NLP Processing
extracts intent and contextual entities.
Lambda Orchestration
transforms intent into an executable action.
Instruction Engine
generates operational guidance.
The system behaves as a deterministic command pipeline: audio input becomes structured intent, which becomes actionable guidance.
This project models a Raspberry Pi–based greenhouse controller as a
deterministic, state-aware automation engine

. Rather than treating it as a simple GPIO script, the system is structured as a continuous feedback loop that measures environmental conditions and translates them into deliberate actuator decisions.

At runtime, the controller continuously reads telemetry from a DHT11 sensor and evaluates those readings against configuration defined by the active growth mode. Each cycle performs threshold evaluation and mutates relay state only when necessary, maintaining environmental stability without unpredictable side effects.
The architecture separates concerns into clearly defined layers:
Hardware Layer:
Raspberry Pi, DHT11 sensor, and GPIO-driven relays interfacing with physical systems.
Configuration Layer:
Growth modes encapsulate humidity thresholds, light schedules, and ventilation policy as data rather than embedded logic.
Control Engine:
A rule-based evaluation function that interprets environmental input and produces deterministic plug state updates.

By externalizing growth strategies into declarative mode objects, the controller remains generic and extensible. Adding a new cultivation strategy does not require modifying the decision engine—only supplying a new configuration structure.
The system functions as a closed feedback loop: measure → evaluate → actuate → repeat. This mental model keeps the automation predictable, transparent, and resilient over long-running execution.

The result is a stable embedded control system that runs indefinitely until terminated, continuously steering the greenhouse environment toward its ideal operating conditions while remaining explicit in its state transitions and configuration boundaries.

Sensor([DHT11 Sensor])
Controller[Sensor Controller]
Settings[Growth Mode Configuration]
Logic[Deterministic Decision Engine]
ChangeCheck{State Change Required?}
Delay[Stability Delay 10s]
StateCompare{Desired ≠ Current?}
Apply[Apply Relay Update]
SensorPipeline[Sensor Evaluation Pipeline]
ControlLoop[Device State Management]

%% --------------------------------------------------
%% SENSOR + EVALUATION PIPELINE
%% --------------------------------------------------

  direction RL

  Sensor --> Controller
  Controller --> Settings
  Settings --> Logic

end

%% DEVICE CONTROL LOOP
%% --------------------------------------------------
subgraph ControlLoop
  direction RL
  Logic --> ChangeCheck


  ChangeCheck -->|No| Delay
  ChangeCheck -->|Yes| StateCompare

  StateCompare -->|No| Delay
  StateCompare -->|Yes| Apply

  Apply --> Delay
  Delay --> SensorPipeline

end

children.text: Autonomous environmental control architecture.
The greenhouse automation system operates as a deterministic feedback engine that continuously senses environmental conditions and applies corrective actions without manual intervention.
Sensing Layer
– A DHT11 sensor provides periodic temperature and humidity readings to the controller.
Policy / Decision Layer
– Threshold logic evaluates readings against growth-mode configuration to determine required adjustments.
Actuation Layer
– GPIO-driven relays toggle physical devices including ventilation, cooling, heating, and humidity control.
By isolating sensing, policy evaluation, and device actuation into distinct layers, the system enforces a clear architectural boundary between hardware I/O and environmental decision logic.
Once configured, the controller maintains environmental stability through a continuous closed-loop regulation model.

sequenceDiagram

participant Sensor as DHT11 Sensor
participant Controller as Control Loop
participant Logic as Threshold Evaluator
participant Relays as GPIO Relay Board
participant Devices as Environmental Devices

loop Continuous Control Cycle
Controller ->> Sensor: Poll temperature + humidity
Sensor -->> Controller: Sensor readings
Controller ->> Logic: Evaluate thresholds

alt Temperature >= Max
Logic -->> Controller: Overheat
Controller ->> Relays: Enable vent + fan
Relays ->> Devices: Activate cooling
else Temperature <= Min
Logic -->> Controller: Undercool
Controller ->> Relays: Disable vent + fan
Relays ->> Devices: Reduce airflow
else Temperature within range
Logic -->> Controller: Temp stable
Controller ->> Relays: Adjust humidity if needed
Relays ->> Devices: Apply humidity change
end
end

children.text: This diagram shows the
client-side encryption pipeline
: UI input → mode selection → alphabet version + magic number → per-character rotation → tag append.
Everything runs locally in the browser—no server dependency—so the original plaintext never leaves the device.
Mode resolution:
User input determines whether encryption or decryption logic is executed.
Cipher configuration:
A random magic number and alphabet version (v1–v9) are selected per operation.
Character rotation:
Each character is rotated using position-aware alphabet mapping.
Self-describing output:
Encryption metadata is appended as a tag to enable automatic future decryption.

subgraph Client["Browser (Client-Side Execution)"]
  direction TB

  %% --------------------------------------------------

    direction LR
    Input[Plaintext Input]
    Trigger[Encrypt Action]

  end

  Input --> Trigger --> Mode

    direction LR
    Version[Alphabet Version v1-v9]
    Magic[Random Magic Number]
  end

  Mode --> Version
  Mode --> Magic

  %% --------------------------------------------------
  %% TRANSFORMATION LAYER
  %% --------------------------------------------------
  subgraph Transformation["Deterministic Rotation Engine"]
    Rotate["Position-Aware Character Rotation"]
  end

  Version --> Rotate
  Magic --> Rotate

  %% --------------------------------------------------
  %% METADATA + OUTPUT
  %% --------------------------------------------------
  subgraph OutputStage["Self-Describing Output"]
    Tag["Append Encryption Tag"]
    Ciphertext["Encrypted Text Output"]
  end

  Rotate --> Tag --> Ciphertext

end

children.text: This diagram focuses purely on the
decryption path
. The system detects the encryption tag, extracts the embedded metadata, resolves the correct alphabet version, and reverses the rotation per character.
The tag contains just enough information to make decryption deterministic — no stored keys or server lookups required.
This sequence diagram illustrates how Enigma
automatically detects and reverses encrypted input
using the metadata embedded inside the encryption tag.
The UI detects the presence of an encryption tag in the pasted input.
The tag parser extracts the alphabet version and magic number.
The version resolver selects the correct alphabet set (v1–v9).
The cipher engine reverses the per-character rotation deterministically.
The decrypted result is rendered immediately with no configuration required.
Decryption is intentionally frictionless — users never need to remember how encryption was performed.

title: Narrative Domain Architecture

children.text: The campaign system is modeled as a hierarchical domain with clearly bounded aggregates. Each layer owns its structural scope while referencing subordinate narrative components.
Storybook acts as the aggregate root, ensuring consistency across acts, rooms, and encounters.

classDef layerExternal fill:#a88900,stroke:#a88900,stroke-width:2px,color:#cBc8ff;

%% Common node types
classDef datastore fill:#000000,stroke:#D1D1D6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;
classDef core fill:#C9A227,stroke:#E6C767,stroke-width:3px,color:#0F0F12;

subgraph external["External Services"]
  StockAPI((Stock Data API))
end

class StockAPI layerExternal

subgraph application["Application State"]
  AppState[Central State Store]
end

class AppState layerApplication

subgraph persistence["Persistence Layer"]
  Firebase((Firebase Database))
end

class Firebase layerPersistence

subgraph presentation["Presentation Layer"]
  ChartUI[Chart Interface]
  MemeUI[Meme Generator]
end

class ChartUI,MemeUI layerPresentation

StockAPI --> AppState
Firebase --> AppState
AppState --> ChartUI
MemeUI --> Firebase

flowchart TB

%% ==========================================================
%% Architecture Layer Palette (Mermaid-safe)
%% - hex colors only (no rgba)
%% - integer px only
%% - avoid rx/ry (parser inconsistencies)
%% ==========================================================

%% Presentation
classDef layerPresentation fill:#1F2793,stroke:#575EB4,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;

%% Application (glass)
classDef layerApplication fill:#1C1C1E,stroke:#D1D1D6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;

%% Domain (gold)
classDef layerDomain fill:#C9A227,stroke:#E6C767,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F0F12;

%% Infrastructure
classDef layerInfrastructure fill:#0F0F12,stroke:#3A3A3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;

%% Persistence (success)
classDef layerPersistence fill:#147832,stroke:#147832,stroke-width:2px,color:#cBc8ff;

%% External (warning)
classDef layerExternal fill:#a88900,stroke:#a88900,stroke-width:2px,color:#cBc8ff;

%% Common node types
classDef datastore fill:#000000,stroke:#D1D1D6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;
classDef core fill:#C9A227,stroke:#E6C767,stroke-width:3px,color:#0F0F12;
subgraph Input["Input Domain"]
  Survey[("Survey Response Data")]
end
subgraph Engine["Scoring Engine"]
  Normalize["Normalization Module"]
  Features["Feature Vector Model"]
  Weighting["Weight Matrix"]
  Aggregate["Compatibility Aggregator"]
end
subgraph Output["Output Domain"]
  Ranking["Ranking Resolver"]
  Explanation["Transparency Module"]
end
Input --> Engine
Engine --> Output
Output --> Input
